<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm these are pure data files with no runtime imports — correctly scoped as isolated test infrastructure
- The fixture set (config.json, prd.json, workflow.md, execution-log.jsonl) mirrors the full runtime state structure, enabling realistic e2e validation
- Keeping fixtures in a dedicated subdirectory (tests/e2e/fixtures/) prevents test data from polluting the source graph
- All four files are runtime state snapshots with no code imports — the zone correctly represents a frozen test environment rather than executable infrastructure.
- The inclusion of execution-log.jsonl alongside prd.json provides test coverage for incremental update scenarios, not just initial state, which increases e2e fidelity.
- Zone name ends in '-fixtures' rather than '-tests' per naming convention since these are data files supporting tests, not test files themselves — naming is appropriate.
- The fixture set's structure (config.json + prd.json + workflow.md + execution-log.jsonl) mirrors the complete runtime state contract of the rex package. Any change to the rex state schema that breaks these fixtures will surface immediately in e2e tests — the zone acts as a schema contract checkpoint enforced at test time.
- rex-e2e-fixtures encodes the full rex runtime state schema as frozen test data. Because all four files are zero-import, changes to the schema must be manually propagated to the fixtures — there is no automated sync. Consider adding a fixture-freshness assertion in e2e tests to detect schema drift before it causes silent test-pass false positives.
- The four fixture files encode the complete rex runtime state schema (prd.json, config.json, workflow.md, execution-log.jsonl) as frozen data with no automated schema-version assertion in the e2e suite. If rex's PRD or config schema evolves without updating fixtures, e2e tests may silently pass against stale contracts — a schema-version field check or fixture-validator would close this gap.
- The four fixture files collectively encode the rex runtime state schema at a specific point in time. Because execution-log.jsonl uses JSONL format while all other state files (prd.json, config.json, workflow.md) use JSON or Markdown, the fixture set implicitly tests format heterogeneity in the rex state model — a property that has not been explicitly documented in the fixture directory's README or test comments.
- Add a schema-version assertion to the e2e suite that reads prd.json and config.json from the fixtures directory and validates their top-level structure against the current PRD and config schemas. This closes the silent drift window where schema evolution passes all tests by hitting stale fixture data rather than the current contract.

</insights>
